content.text: Reliable LLM applications need more than prompt engineering — they need programmatic checks that block dangerous inputs, enforce structured outputs, and automatically retry or repair responses that violate business rules. Guardrails AI is an open-source Python library that sits between the caller and the model, running a configurable pipeline of validators on every request and response.
package) is a Python framework that wraps LLM calls with declarative input and output validation. The core abstraction is the
— an object that holds one or more
, specifies what to do when validation fails (raise an exception, silently fix the value, or ask the model to try again), and exposes a
call interface that replaces the raw model call.
Validators can be applied to the full prompt before it reaches the LLM (
) or to the full completion after the model responds (
). For structured outputs, validators can also target individual fields of a Pydantic model. The
— hosts hundreds of pre-built validators covering PII detection, toxicity filtering, JSON schema enforcement, fact-checking, prompt injection detection, and more.

Production LLM systems fail in ways that unit tests and static prompts cannot prevent. Models hallucinate facts, produce invalid JSON when instructions are ambiguous, leak PII they encountered in context, and can be manipulated through adversarial user inputs. Without a validation layer these failures surface silently as downstream bugs — a JSON parse error in an API handler, a compliance incident when PII appears in logs, or an incorrect action taken by an autonomous agent.
even with structured output modes, models occasionally emit extra fields, wrong types, or missing required keys — especially with complex nested schemas.
GDPR, HIPAA, and SOC 2 require that PII is not inadvertently stored or transmitted. Without an automated check, sensitive data surfaces invisibly in completions.

a user-supplied input that contains instructions like "Ignore previous instructions and instead…" can redirect an agent to perform unintended actions. Input guards catch and block these before the model processes them.
output quality degrades under concurrency, model updates, and prompt drift. Validators make quality guarantees explicit and testable.
chain multiple validators on a single Guard; each runs independently and its on_fail action is applied separately, giving fine-grained control without tangled if/else logic.

when an output fails validation, Guardrails can construct a corrective prompt that includes the original output and the error message, re-send it to the model, and return the fixed result — all transparently, without the caller writing retry logic.
install community validators with a single CLI command (
); no need to write PII detection, toxicity classifiers, or JSON validators from scratch.
works with any callable that accepts messages and returns a string, including OpenAI, Anthropic, Azure OpenAI, Cohere, local models via LiteLLM, and custom wrappers.
and Guardrails handles the schema prompt injection, JSON parsing, and field-level validation in a single call, returning a typed Python object.

each validator adds processing time; some Hub validators (toxicity classifiers, embedding-based similarity checks) can add hundreds of milliseconds per call. Reask loops multiply total latency by the number of retries.
every reask is a new LLM call. With
and a model that frequently fails, token costs can quadruple.
Hub validators are community-maintained and vary in quality. Some rely on smaller models that themselves hallucinate, producing false positives or negatives.
streaming responses require buffering chunks until validation can run, which partially defeats the UX benefits of streaming. Partial validation support exists but is limited.
Guardrails AI has gone through several major API redesigns (RAIL XML → Python API → v0.5 Guard API). Projects written against older versions require migration work.

At its core, a Guard is a pipeline: validators run sequentially (or in parallel, depending on configuration), and each validator's outcome determines whether the pipeline continues, retries, or aborts. The guard inspects the raw string output from the model — or a parsed JSON structure if
is a Pydantic model — and applies each validator in turn.
is used when the on_fail action is
— for example, a PII validator might return the text with PII replaced by
Every validator is registered with an on_fail action that controls behaviour when
— replaces the output with fix_value from the validator (no LLM call).
— builds a corrective prompt and calls the LLM again, up to
— removes the failing item from a list (useful for filtering individual array elements).
— logs the failure but continues; useful in monitoring-only mode.

When a validator fails with on_fail=REASK, Guardrails assembles a follow-up message that contains the original model output, the validator's error message, and instructions to fix the specific problem. This reask prompt is appended to the message history and sent back to the model. The cycle repeats up to
times; if the output still fails after all retries, the Guard raises

attrs.alt: Guardrails AI pipeline: User Input passes through Input Guard validators, then to LLM, then through Output Guard validators — failures trigger block or reask retry loop. Hub provides community validators.

content.text: The pipeline diagram shows the two interception points: the
runs before the LLM sees the prompt (blocking bad inputs early and cheaply) and the
runs after the model responds (enforcing format, content, and compliance rules). When an output validator fails and is configured with
, Guardrails constructs a corrective prompt and loops back to the LLM, up to the configured maximum number of retries. Validators are sourced from the Guardrails Hub or authored locally.
when the downstream code expects a Pydantic model (a parsed JSON object) from the model's free-text output, and failures would cause runtime exceptions or data corruption.
medical, financial, and legal applications where PII, PHI, or confidential terms must never appear in model outputs regardless of what the user requests.
when user inputs are untrusted and may include prompt injection attempts, hate speech, or personally harmful requests that must be blocked before reaching the model.
when LLM outputs are used to drive tool calls, database writes, or external API calls — a malformed action argument can cause irreversible side effects; output validation is a last line of defence.

low-latency interactive UX where every millisecond of added overhead is felt; high-volume free-text summarisation where no output schema is required and content policy is enforced upstream; or projects that already use a model provider's built-in structured output mode (which handles JSON schema enforcement natively, without reask overhead).
The following steps cover the full setup from installation to a working guard with Hub validators and custom validators.
Install the package and the Guardrails CLI:
Authenticate with the Hub (free account) and install validators:
Import the Guard and validators, then compose a validation pipeline:
Call the guard instead of the raw LLM function:
Handle ValidationError for blocked inputs and exhausted reasks:

# Step 1 — install
pip install guardrails-ai

# Step 2 — authenticate and install Hub validators
guardrails configure   # enter your API token from hub.guardrailsai.com
guardrails hub install hub://guardrails/toxic_language
guardrails hub install hub://guardrails/detect_pii
guardrails hub install hub://guardrails/valid_json

# Steps 3-5 — compose a Guard and call it

from guardrails import Guard, OnFailAction
from guardrails.hub import ToxicLanguage, DetectPII, ValidJson
import openai

client = openai.OpenAI()

guard = (
    Guard()
    # Input validator — runs before the LLM sees the prompt
    .use(ToxicLanguage, on="prompt", on_fail=OnFailAction.EXCEPTION)
    # Output validators — run after the model responds
    .use(DetectPII, on="output", on_fail=OnFailAction.FIX)   # redact PII in place
    .use(ValidJson, on="output", on_fail=OnFailAction.REASK)  # retry if not valid JSON
)

try:
    result = guard(
        client.chat.completions.create,
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "List 3 capital cities as JSON array."}],
        num_reasks=2,
    )
    print(result.validated_output)   # the validated (and PII-redacted) string
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Guard blocked request: {e}")
Example 1: structured output with a Pydantic model

as a Pydantic model is the most common usage pattern for structured extraction. Guardrails injects a JSON schema instruction into the prompt, parses the completion, and validates each field. The return value is a typed Python object.

from guardrails import Guard, OnFailAction
from guardrails.hub import ValidLength, ValidChoices
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from typing import Literal
import openai

client = openai.OpenAI()

class ProductReview(BaseModel):
    product_name: str = Field(description="Name of the product")
    sentiment: Literal["positive", "negative", "neutral"]
    score: int = Field(ge=1, le=10, description="Rating between 1 and 10")
    summary: str = Field(description="One-sentence summary")

guard = Guard.for_pydantic(output_class=ProductReview)

result = guard(
    client.chat.completions.create,
    model="gpt-4o-mini",
    messages=[
        {"role": "system", "content": "Extract structured product review data."},
        {"role": "user",   "content": "I bought this headphone last week. Sound is great but the ear cups are uncomfortable. Overall 6 out of 10."},
    ],
    num_reasks=2,
)

review: ProductReview = result.validated_output
print(review.sentiment)   # e.g. 'negative' or 'neutral'
print(review.score)       # e.g. 6
Example 2: custom validator for domain-specific rules
When no Hub validator covers a domain-specific constraint, subclass
. The validator can be used locally without publishing to the Hub.
Example 3: async guard for FastAPI endpoints
— it wraps async LLM client methods and awaits them inside the validation pipeline.

from guardrails import AsyncGuard, OnFailAction
from guardrails.hub import ToxicLanguage, DetectPII
from pydantic import BaseModel
from openai import AsyncOpenAI
from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException

app = FastAPI()
aclient = AsyncOpenAI()

class ChatResponse(BaseModel):
    reply: str
    language: str

guard = (
    AsyncGuard.for_pydantic(output_class=ChatResponse)
    .use(ToxicLanguage, on="prompt", on_fail=OnFailAction.EXCEPTION)
    .use(DetectPII, on="output", on_fail=OnFailAction.FIX)
)

@app.post("/chat")
async def chat(user_message: str):
    try:
        result = await guard(
            aclient.chat.completions.create,
            model="gpt-4o-mini",
            messages=[
                {"role": "system", "content": "Respond helpfully. Detect the language."},
                {"role": "user",   "content": user_message},
            ],
            num_reasks=1,
        )
        return result.validated_output   # ChatResponse instance
    except Exception as e:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=400, detail=str(e))

Consider a B2B SaaS product that uses an LLM to generate contract clause summaries from uploaded PDFs. Each clause summary must: (1) be flagged with a risk level, (2) never leak client names or dollar amounts that appear in the context but were not in the relevant clause, and (3) remain within a character limit for a UI badge. The raw model output is frequently correct, but occasionally violates these constraints under load.

from guardrails import Guard, OnFailAction
from guardrails.hub import DetectPII, ValidLength
from guardrails.validators import Validator, register_validator, PassResult, FailResult
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from typing import Literal
import openai

client = openai.OpenAI()

# Domain model for contract clause output
class ClauseSummary(BaseModel):
    clause_type: str = Field(description="Legal clause category, e.g. Indemnification")
    risk_level: Literal["low", "medium", "high", "critical"]
    summary: str = Field(max_length=280, description="Short plain-language summary")
    action_required: bool

@register_validator(name="no-dollar-amounts", data_type="string")
class NoDollarAmounts(Validator):
    """Blocks outputs that contain raw monetary values."""
    import re
    _PAT = re.compile(r"\$[\d,]+(?:\.\d{2})?")

    def validate(self, value, metadata):
        if self._PAT.search(str(value)):
            return FailResult(
                error_message="Summary must not contain dollar amounts.",
                fix_value=self._PAT.sub("[AMOUNT]", str(value)),
            )
        return PassResult()

guard = (
    Guard.for_pydantic(output_class=ClauseSummary)
    .use(DetectPII, on="output", on_fail=OnFailAction.FIX)   # redact leaked client names
    .use(NoDollarAmounts, on="output", on_fail=OnFailAction.FIX)
)

def summarise_clause(clause_text: str) -> ClauseSummary:
    result = guard(
        client.chat.completions.create,
        model="gpt-4o",
        messages=[
            {"role": "system", "content": "Summarise the following contract clause as JSON."},
            {"role": "user",   "content": clause_text},
        ],
        num_reasks=2,
        temperature=0.1,
    )
    return result.validated_output  # typed ClauseSummary, PII and amounts redacted

At scale this pipeline processes 50,000 clauses per day across hundreds of concurrent workers. The PII validator catches client name leakage in roughly 0.3% of responses; the dollar-amount validator fires on about 0.8%. Most failures are auto-fixed without reask, so the actual reask rate stays below 0.1% — acceptable latency overhead.

REASK is expensive. If a validator can auto-correct the output (e.g., strip bad characters, redact PII), prefer FIX — it adds no LLM round-trip. Reserve REASK for validators where the fix requires semantic understanding (e.g., a missing required field).

running a toxicity classifier, an embedding-based similarity check, and a fact-checking validator on every call adds seconds of latency. Profile each validator independently and apply them only where the business risk justifies the cost.
Ignoring the validated_output vs raw_llm_output distinction:
is what passed (or was fixed by) the validators. Always use
bypasses the entire point of the guard.
too broadly and returning an empty string on failure hides guard activations from logging and monitoring. Catch
specifically and emit a structured log event so the failure rate is observable.
Forgetting input validators for agentic tools:
most teams add output guards but omit input guards. If an agent's tool input is untrusted user data, prompt injection can redirect the agent even before the LLM processes it.

guard behaviour depends on the combination of validators, the model, and the prompt. Changes to any of these can silently alter which inputs pass or fail. Run integration tests that exercise both valid and invalid inputs through the guard.
apply cheap structural validators (regex, length, JSON schema) before expensive semantic validators (embedding similarity, LLM-based classifiers). Short-circuit early on cheap failures.
Use NOOP in staging to measure failure rates:
in pre-production to quantify how often they fire before switching to EXCEPTION or REASK in production.
a Guard object is thread-safe for concurrent calls once constructed. Build it once at startup (e.g., as a module-level singleton or a dependency in FastAPI) and reuse it across requests.
, and the list of failed validators in
are valuable signals for dashboarding and alerting.
Hub validators follow semver. Pin to a specific version in requirements (e.g.,
) to prevent silent behaviour changes from upstream releases.
Prefer native structured output modes for pure JSON extraction:
if the only validation requirement is JSON schema conformance, OpenAI's
or Anthropic's tool-use mode is lower latency and zero reask overhead. Use Guardrails on top when you need additional semantic validators beyond schema conformance.

Guard overhead is dominated by validator execution time. Pure-Python validators (regex, JSON parsing, length checks) add under 1 ms. Model-based validators — toxicity classifiers that load a local transformer, or embedding-based grounding checks — can add 50–300 ms per call depending on hardware. Running these on CPU for high-throughput services is the most common bottleneck.

At scale, isolate heavy validators into a separate microservice fronted by a message queue. The LLM response is written to a queue; the validator service processes it asynchronously and writes results back. The caller receives the raw response immediately and the validated/redacted version slightly later. This pattern trades immediate consistency for throughput and is appropriate when a sub-second validation delay is acceptable (e.g., an async document processing pipeline, not a synchronous chat turn).
Reask depth is the second scaling concern. With
and a 5% raw failure rate, the expected additional LLM calls per request is 0.05 + 0.05

0.05 ≈ 0.053 extra calls, tolerable at most volumes. At a 30% raw failure rate with 3 reasks, expected overhead rises to roughly 0.58 extra calls per request — nearly doubling token spend. Monitor the failure rate per validator and reduce
or switch to FIX/REFRAIN if rates are high.
pip install guardrails-ai; v0.5+ uses the Guard/AsyncGuard API
hub.guardrailsai.com; install validators with guardrails hub install
guardrails configure, guardrails hub install, guardrails hub list
Pass litellm.completion as the LLM callable to use any provider
Used by Hub's DetectPII validator; requires presidio-analyzer and spaCy model
Guard.for_pydantic() accepts BaseModel subclasses; field validators run alongside Guardrails validators
Mixed content + schema validation, hub ecosystem
Full control, zero deps, high maintenance

Guardrails AI is itself a Python library — its own code must be treated as a trusted dependency. Validators loaded from the Hub execute arbitrary Python, so review Hub validator source code before installing, especially in regulated environments. Use
or Dependabot to track CVEs in guardrails-ai and its transitive dependencies (notably Presidio, spaCy, and transformer models).

a determined adversary can craft inputs that pass current validators but still cause harm. Guardrails reduces risk — it is not a complete security boundary. Combine it with provider-side content moderation and application-level rate limiting.

the Presidio-based PII validator uses NLP entity recognition; unusual name formats, obfuscated values, or non-English PII may escape detection. For high-assurance GDPR compliance, add a deterministic regex layer on top.

the reask message includes the model's previous (failed) output. If that output contains adversarial instructions, they are fed back into the model's context. Sanitise the error message included in the reask prompt or use a separate system turn that does not include raw model output.
Guard as a single point of failure:
if a validator raises an unhandled exception (e.g., a model-based validator's remote service is down), the entire guard call fails. Wrap validator calls in try/except inside the
with a logged warning when a non-critical validator is unavailable.

for compliance, persist the raw LLM output, the list of validators that fired, and the final validated output. This creates a tamper-evident record that validation ran and what it changed, useful for incident investigation and regulatory audit.
wraps any LLM callable and runs a pipeline of validators on the prompt and/or completion before returning a result — no changes to the underlying model call are needed.

On-fail actions (FIX, REASK, EXCEPTION, FILTER, REFRAIN, NOOP) give fine-grained control over what happens when a validator fails — choose based on whether the fix requires semantic understanding (REASK) or is deterministic (FIX).

provides installable validators for the most common needs (PII, toxicity, JSON schema, prompt injection) — write custom validators only for domain-specific rules not covered by existing Hub validators.
REASK is powerful but expensive: each retry is a full LLM round-trip. Monitor per-validator failure rates and prefer FIX for validators that can deterministically correct their failure cases.
— using the raw output bypasses all validation and defeats the purpose of the guard.

For pure JSON schema extraction, provider-native structured output modes or Instructor are lower latency alternatives; Guardrails AI adds the most value when semantic validators (PII, toxicity, factuality) are needed alongside schema enforcement.

Treat Guardrails as a defence-in-depth layer, not a complete security perimeter: validators can be bypassed by sufficiently adversarial inputs, so combine them with upstream content moderation, rate limiting, and audit logging.
